metadata.title: Every valley is a parabola if you lean in close enough

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The Morse well and its fitted parabola merging below 0.05 nanometers made 'every minimum lies' land hard for me. I want to run the Taylor argument on a real bond. Can you (1) expand a Morse potential about its equilibrium and show the linear term vanishing because the first derivative is zero, (2) extract the effective spring constant from the second derivative and turn it into an equally-spaced level spacing, and (3) show me roughly where the anharmonicity becomes large enough that the parabola prediction visibly fails?

props.command: The Morse well and its fitted parabola merging below 0.05 nanometers made 'every minimum lies' land hard for me. I want to run the Taylor argument on a real bond. Can you (1) expand a Morse potential about its equilibrium and show the linear term vanishing because the first derivative is zero, (2) extract the effective spring constant from the second derivative and turn it into an equally-spaced level spacing, and (3) show me roughly where the anharmonicity becomes large enough that the parabola prediction visibly fails?
props.segment: Every valley is a parabola if

beats.narration_text: The quantum harmonic oscillator is everywhere in physics. Molecular bonds, crystal lattices, trapped atoms — all of them. Why does one simple model fit everything?

card.dek: Every valley is a parabola if you lean in close enough

beats.narration_text: Nature is full of complicated, lopsided potential wells. A perfect parabola predicts quantum energy levels equally spaced. Real wells are not parabolas. Yet the equally-spaced levels keep showing up. Why?

card.dek: Complicated wells predict complicated spectra. Parabola predicts equal spacing. Real spectra show equal spacing. Why?

beats.narration_text: A potential well can have any shape — asymmetric, lopsided, with cusps and bumps. Any smooth function drawn over a minimum.
Taylor-expand the potential about its minimum. The first derivative is zero — that is what a minimum means. The linear term dies. What is left is a quadratic.
Zoom into the bottom of any smooth well. Watch the well and a fitted parabola approach each other as the region narrows. At the minimum, they merge.
The effective spring constant is the second derivative of the potential at the minimum. That is the only number the parabola needs. The shape everywhere else is irrelevant for small oscillations.
Every molecular bond, every lattice site, every trapped atom — each has its own complicated potential. But near the bottom, each becomes a parabola with its own spring constant.
The harmonic oscillator is universal not because nature is simple. It is universal because every minimum lies.

props.headline: The harmonic oscillator is universal not because nature is simple. It…

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: a Morse potential for a diatomic bond. Far from equilibrium it is asymmetric and anharmonic. Zoom to the bottom: the well and its fitted parabola agree for displacements under 0.05 nanometers.
The linear term dies at any minimum. What remains is a quadratic — a parabola. Zoom close enough and every well becomes harmonic. That is why the harmonic oscillator is everywhere.

card.dek: Linear term = 0 at minimum. Leading survivor = quadratic. Every well is a parabola up close.

Zoom to the bottom: the well and its fitted parabola agree for displacements under 0.05 nanometers.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
